title: SEGMENT 6 — Human blastoids: Polo, Hanna, and the 2021 papers

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — Human blastoids: Polo, Hanna, and the 2021 papers

In March 2021, two papers appeared back-to-back in Nature that, more than any other publications in the field, brought synthetic embryos out of the specialist literature and into the public conversation.
One was from Jose Polo's lab at Monash University in Australia.
The other was from Jacob Hanna's lab at the Weizmann Institute in Israel.
Both papers reported the creation of human blastoids — blastocyst-like structures, made from human stem cells, that recapitulated the organization of a real human blastocyst.
The Polo paper used human induced pluripotent stem cells as the starting point.

Through a careful sequence of culture conditions, the iPSCs were pushed to generate the three lineages of the early embryo — epiblast-like cells, trophectoderm-like cells, and hypoblast-like cells — in a single integrated process.

The cells were aggregated in microwells, and within a few days they self-organized into structures that had the three lineages in the right positions, a central cavity, and the right overall size and shape.
Human blastoids.
From iPSCs.
No real embryo involved at any step.
The Hanna paper, published in the same issue, took a different but converging approach.

Hanna's group had been working for years on naive human pluripotent stem cells — a particular state of pluripotency that more closely resembles the inner cell mass of a blastocyst than the more conventional "primed" state of most human ES and iPSC lines.

From naive cells, his group could derive both epiblast-like and trophoblast-like progeny in a single system, and combine them — along with hypoblast-like cells — into self-organizing blastocyst-like structures.
Same general result, slightly different route.
Human blastoids made from stem cells alone.
Why did these papers cause a stir?
Several reasons.
First, the structures were good.
Under the microscope, a human blastoid from either lab is hard to distinguish from a real human blastocyst at first glance.
The size is roughly right — about 200 microns.
The cavity is right.
The cell type distribution is roughly right — outer trophectoderm-like cells, inner cell mass-like cluster, hypoblast-like layer at the boundary.
The molecular markers match.
Single-cell sequencing showed that the three populations in the blastoid expressed roughly the right genes at roughly the right levels.
Second, the structures were testable.
You could, in principle, ask whether a human blastoid could implant.

Polo and Hanna both tested this with in vitro implantation models — taking the blastoid and putting it on a layer of endometrial cells, or in conditions that mimic the early uterine environment, and watching whether it attached and started to organize as an implantation event.
To some degree, yes.
The blastoids attached.
They began to flatten and spread.
They started behaving like implanting embryos.

They were never transferred to actual human uteruses — that would have been ethically and legally impermissible — but the in vitro implantation experiments showed that the blastoids had functional, not just morphological, similarity to real blastocysts.
Third, the policy implications were immediate.
Within weeks of the papers, ethics committees, professional societies, and regulatory bodies were grappling with what to do about human blastoids.
Were they human embryos for legal purposes?
The answer, in most jurisdictions, was unclear, because the laws had been written for embryos created by fertilization, and these structures were created from stem cells.

The International Society for Stem Cell Research, the ISSCR, was finalizing its 2021 guidelines just as the papers came out, and the timing was not coincidental — the guidelines had to address synthetic embryos directly, and they did.
The Hanna and Polo papers also illustrated something important about the state of the technology.
Efficiency is low.
Only a small fraction of starting aggregates — typically less than ten percent, sometimes much less — develop into properly organized blastoids.
The rest are misshapen, missing one or more lineages, or just disorganized clumps.
The papers reported their results by selecting the morphologically correct blastoids for analysis.
This is honest practice — you have to specify what counts as a successful blastoid — but it also means that the headline numbers can obscure the noisiness of the actual experiments.
Fidelity is imperfect.
Even the best human blastoids aren't perfect copies of real blastocysts.
Single-cell sequencing has shown subtle differences in gene expression between blastoid cells and real blastocyst cells.
The proportions of cell types can be off.
Some markers are expressed too strongly, others not strongly enough.
The blastoids are recognizable as models of blastocysts, but they're not indistinguishable.

This matters scientifically — if you're using a blastoid to study a particular biological process, you need to know how well that process is recapitulated — and it matters ethically, because the imperfections are part of why many researchers argue blastoids should not be treated as full equivalents of real embryos.
Variability is high between labs.
Different protocols give different blastoids.
Some protocols give better trophoblast representation, others better epiblast, others better cavity formation.
The field is still standardizing, still figuring out which differences in protocol matter and which don't.
A blastoid from one lab in 2021 was not necessarily the same as a blastoid from another lab in 2022.
These caveats matter.
The headline — "scientists make human embryos from stem cells" — is technically accurate but glosses over the imperfections that make blastoids more a useful model than a true equivalent.
We'll come back to these limitations in Segment 13.
But the proof of concept is real.
In 2021, two labs independently showed that you could take human iPSCs, run them through a defined protocol, and end up with structures that look and partly behave like human blastocysts.
That fact, by itself, changed what was possible.
The next question — and it was asked immediately — was: can we go further?
Can we keep these structures developing past the blastocyst stage?
Can we model implantation, gastrulation, early organogenesis?
In humans, in vitro, from stem cells?
The answer, as we'll see in Segment 7 and Segment 11, was yes — at least partially.
And the lab that pushed furthest was the same Hanna lab in Israel that had co-authored the 2021 human blastoid paper.
